ETC5512: Instructions for Tutorial 8

Emi Tanaka

Week 8 Australian election data

🎯 Objectives

In this tutorial, you will learn to

  • work with map data and visualise it
  • integrate information from another source

🔧 Preparation

Note: you should do Preparation before the tutorial.

  1. Installing relevant R-package
install.packages("sf")
  1. Get the distribution of preferences by candidate by division for the 2019 Australian Federal Election
    • Go to https://results.aec.gov.au
      • 2019 federal election
      • Downloads
      • Distribution of preferences by candidate by division
    • Or directly here: https://results.aec.gov.au/24310/Website/Downloads/HouseDopByDivisionDownload-24310.csv
  2. Getting the electoral district geographical boundaries
    • Go to https://www.aec.gov.au/electorates/gis/licence.htm to agree with the license
    • Download the ESRI (.shp) zip file for Victoria.
  3. Getting the SA1 geographical boundaries
    • Go to https://datapacks.censusdata.abs.gov.au/geopackages/ selecting the following options:
      • Employment, Income and Unpaid Work (EIUW)
      • EIUW GeoPackage A

🤺 Exercise 8A

Map the election data

We will draw a map of Victoria and colour the electorate districts with the political party that won that district in the 2019 federal election.

  • First, import the election data and map data as below.
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
election2019 <- read_csv("https://results.aec.gov.au/24310/Website/Downloads/HouseDopByDivisionDownload-24310.csv", 
                             skip = 1) %>% 
   # select winning candidate only 
   filter(CalculationType == "Preference Count" & 
             Elected == "Y" & 
             CountNumber == 0) %>% 
   # make division name upper case so 
   # it can be joined later with map data
   mutate(DivisionNm = toupper(DivisionNm))
vic_map <- read_sf(here::here("data/vic-july-2018-esri/E_AUGFN3_region.shp")) %>% 
   # to match up with election data
   mutate(DivisionNm = toupper(Elect_div)) 
# define the political colors (feel free to change)
party_colors <- c("ALP" = "#DE3533", "LNP" = "#ADD8E6", 
                  "KAP" = "#8B0000", "GRN" = "#10C25B", 
                  "GVIC" = "#10C25B", "XEN" = "#ff6300", 
                  "LP" = "#0047AB", "NP" = "#0a9cca", 
                  "IND" = "#000000")
  • Draw a map that looks like below. The map shows the coordinates with longitude of 144.8 to 145.2 and latitude of -38.1 to -37.6.

Solution

ggplot(left_join(vic_map, election2019, by = "DivisionNm")) +
   geom_sf(aes(geometry = geometry, fill = PartyAb), 
                  color = "white") + 
   coord_sf(xlim = c(144.8, 145.2), ylim = c(-38.1, -37.6)) +
   scale_fill_manual(values = party_colors) + 
   ggtitle("Winners of Australian Federal Election in 2019", 
           subtitle = "Victoria")

🏂 Exercise 8B

Integrate data from different sources

  • Import the GeoPackage data with SA1 regions.
SA1map <- read_sf(here::here("data/Geopackage_2016_EIUWA_for_VIC/census2016_eiuwa_vic_short.gpkg"), 
                  layer = "census2016_eiuwa_vic_sa1_short")
  • Calculate the centroids for each SA1 region by completing the code below. What is each function doing below?
SA1map <- SA1map %>% 
   mutate(centroid = map(..., ~setNames(st_centroid(.x), c("x", "y")))) %>%
               unnest_wider(...)
  • Plot the median weekly personal income data on top of the electoral boundaries like below. Can you find which regions have wealthy individuals?

  • Looking at the map in 8A, there is one electorate won by the Green party. Looking at the map above, this is where a lot of wealthy individuals live. Can you say that those who vote for the Green party are rich individuals? Why or why not? Discuss with your classmate.
  • Why do some geographical regions look sparse in terms of the census reported median weekly personal income?
  • What is ecological fallacy? How does it relate to your conclusion from before?
